coredata.dc:title: A carbon quantum dot-encapsulated micellar reactor for the synthesis of chromene derivatives in water
coredata.dc:creator: Mayank,
Singh, Amanpreet
Kaur, Navneet
Singh, Narinder
Jang, Doo Ok
coredata.dc:description: An efficient protocol for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives via a one-pot multicomponent reaction was developed using a self-assembled catalyst system. The self-assembling nature of the fabricated catalyst is unique, consisting of carbon-quantum dots (CQDs) and ionic liquids (ILs). At the critical micelle concentration (CMC), the ILs organize themselves as micelles and the CQDs are trapped inside, forming a CQD-colloidal reaction system (CRS). The reactions of naphthols with aromatic aldehydes and malononitrile in the CQD-CRS system gave excellent yields of 4H-chromene derivatives at room temperature in 15min upon sonication. The present method provides a high EcoScale score and a low E-factor, validating the greenness of the procedure. The self-aggregating nature of the catalyst is highly advantageous as it can be recycled and reused provided the CMC is maintained. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of a self-assembled CQD-based catalyst in aqueous media. CQD-CRS can be considered a designer’s catalyst, as by changing the ILs/CQDs components, multiple variants could be produced under different solvent conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Ionic liquid
Micellar reactor
4H-chromene derivative
Water
Green chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation of a CQD-CRS hybrid system
Typical procedure for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectrum of CQDs; (B) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (C) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs; (D) TGA of CQDs; (E) PXRD of CQDs; (F) TEM images of CQDs.

Fluorescence emission profiles of CQDs upon addition of different concentrations of (A) IL-1 and (B) IL-2; AFM images of (C) IL-1-based micelles, (D) CQD-CRS formed by IL-1, (E) IL-2-based micelles, and (F) CQD-CRS formed by IL-2; DLS size distribution of (G) CQD-CRS formed by IL-1 and (H) CQD-CRS formed by IL-2.
ORTEP diagrams of compounds (A) 7 and (B) 8 with 40% probable thermal ellipsoids.
Model reaction for evaluating the catalytic ability of CQD-CRS.
Plausible mechanism for the synthesis of 4H-chromenes in CQD-CRS.
Efficiency of the reaction depending on ILs and reaction times.
Effect of different concentrations of ILs and CQDs on the yield of the product.
Effect of the temperature on the yield of the product.
Synthesis of various 4H-chromene derivatives starting from naphthalen-1-ol using CQD-CRS.
Synthesis of various 4H-chromene derivatives starting from naphthalen-2-ol using CQD-CRS.

head.ce:title: A carbon quantum dot-encapsulated micellar reactor for the synthesis of chromene derivatives in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute Technology, Ropar, Punjab 140001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yonsei University, Wonju 26493, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient protocol for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives via a one-pot multicomponent reaction was developed using a self-assembled catalyst system. The self-assembling nature of the fabricated catalyst is unique, consisting of carbon-quantum dots (CQDs) and ionic liquids (ILs). At the critical micelle concentration (CMC), the ILs organize themselves as micelles and the CQDs are trapped inside, forming a CQD-colloidal reaction system (CRS). The reactions of naphthols with aromatic aldehydes and malononitrile in the CQD-CRS system gave excellent yields of 4H-chromene derivatives at room temperature in 15min upon sonication. The present method provides a high EcoScale score and a low E-factor, validating the greenness of the procedure. The self-aggregating nature of the catalyst is highly advantageous as it can be recycled and reused provided the CMC is maintained. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of a self-assembled CQD-based catalyst in aqueous media. CQD-CRS can be considered a designer’s catalyst, as by changing the ILs/CQDs components, multiple variants could be produced under different solvent conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Multi-component reactions (MCRs) are a fascinating class of organic reactions that involve the participation of three or more reactants in a convergent manner to yield minimum amounts of side products [1]. The history of MCRs started with the Strecker synthesis of α-amino cyanides. Recently, this research area has been flourishing with the documentation of up to seven-component MCRs involving the coinciding participation of the Ugi and Asinger reactions [2,3]. From a kinetic point of view, synthetic chemists may be discouraged to develop such reactions, since on the basis of the collision theory, the simultaneous interaction of three or more reactants may result in low reaction rates and eventually poor yields. On the other hand, these reactions have received great attention as the synthetic strategies are designed in such a way that the combination of the reactants occurs in a sequential manner to yield the products with high selectivity. Furthermore, the products retain the majority of the atoms of the starting materials authenticating the high atom economy of these strategies and conforming the principles of green chemistry [4]. To attain the required goal of the synthetic strategy and overcome the kinetic limitations, several efforts have been reported to fine-tune the reaction conditions, including the solvent, temperature, catalyst, and concentration in addition to the nature of the starting materials and orientation of the functional groups [5,6].

Extensive use of metal-based catalysts and toxic solvents is continuously increasing environmental pollution, resulting in an alarming concern for living systems [7]. Minimizing the use of metal-based catalysts in chemical reactions is important because these metals are toxic and their proper disposal is complicated [7]. Furthermore, retention of small quantities of transition metals in pharmaceutical products may lead to severe toxic effects in the living population [7]. Toxic and volatile organic solvents used in synthetic procedures are also contributing to the chemical waste, imparting serious deteriorative effects to the environment [8]. To some extent, the source of energy employed during the reactions is also an important factor to be considered Thus, implementation of environmentally acceptable chemicals, including catalysts and solvents, together with the utilization of safer forms of energy sources, has emerged as a major goal in green chemistry.

Water is a highly attractive solvent from an economical and environmental point of view because it is cheap, readily available, non-flammable, and non-toxic [9]. Moreover, the unique structure and physicochemical properties associated with water result in the possibility of specific interactions such as polar, hydrophobic, trans-phase, and hydrogen bonding interactions that can significantly influence reactions. However, the major problem associated with the use of water as solvent for organic transformations is its low solubilizing ability towards most organic compounds. To overcome this issue, several strategies has been implemented, including the use of salts to impart hydrophobicity to the aqueous systems and utilization of specially designed catalysts such as cyclodextrins, microgel-based formulations, and micelles formed from organic cations [8,10,11].

As far as the metal-free catalysts are concerned, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as versatile metal-free entities with a wide range of properties in terms of catalytic applications [12]. Kang et al. demonstrated the aldol condensation of acetone with aldehydes in the presence of CQDs [13]. CQD-coated nanoparticles were found to be efficient for Heck and Suzuki coupling reactions [14]. A CQD-mediated catalysis was reported for the formation of spiro/glycoquinazolinones [15]. Besides their catalytic abilities, additional attributes associated with these materials include their environmentally friendly chemical composition, non-toxicity, high stability, and reusability [16].

In this context, we have designed an MCR for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives employing CQDs and ionic liquids (ILs). It was envisaged that CQDs could play the role of a metal-free catalyst, while the ILs could impart the solubility to the reactants by forming micelles [17]. The 4H-chromene moiety is an integral part of many heterocyclic compounds, which are responsible for many biological activities such as anticancer, antimicrobial, antiviral, antioxidant, hypotensive, anticoagulant, diuretic, and anti-allergic activities [18,19]. The majority of the reported methods for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives employed non-recyclable metal catalysts and harsh reaction conditions, which are unsuitable for industrial applications as summarized in Table S1 (Supplementary Information). Herein, we report an efficient and environmentally friendly MCR for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives under mild reaction conditions.

The typical pyrolysis technique was employed to produce CQDs [20]. An aqueous solution of citric acid was placed in a Teflon autoclave and kept at 160°C for 6h. During the course of the reaction, the citric acid underwent pyrolysis, affording uniformly dispersed CQDs that were further purified by centrifugation followed by dialysis.

The characterization of CQDs was performed using the UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), differential light scattering (DLS), and powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of CQDs exhibited a couple of absorption bands (Fig. 1 A). The absorption band at 340nm could be assigned to the π-π* transition, while the other band at 245nm was attributed to the n-π* electronic transition [20]. Based on the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the band gap of CQDs was calculated using a Tauc plot, and it was found to be 5.35eV (Fig. S1A). The quantum confinement effect was observed, which makes the energy level discrete, increasing the band gap of the material [21]. The fluorescence profiles of CQDs at variable excitation wavelengths showed that their fluorescence intensity is highly dependent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1B). The tunable fluorescence emission is a characteristic of CQDs, confirming the formation of CQDs [22,23]. The surface properties of CQDs are critical because the catalytic ability of the derived materials is highly dependent on them. The surface functional groups of CQDs were examined by IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1C). A couple of high intensity bands were observed, including a broad band at 3500–2500cm−1 and another at 1650cm−1. The former peak was assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, while the peak at 1650cm−1 was typically assigned to the carbonyl groups [24]. The surface coverage with OH and COOH groups improves the hydrophilicity and dispersibility of CQDs in water. To estimate surface functional group density as well as the pyrolytic behavior of CQDs at high temperature, TGA was performed by heating the sample up to 700°C under a N2 atmosphere (Fig. 1D). The mass loss at temperatures higher than 121°C was significant, which represents the high compactness of functionalities on the surface of CQDs [20]. The powder XRD pattern of CQDs was recorded. A single peak was observed at the position corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 3.77Å (Fig. 1E). Interestingly, the interlayer distance was greater than that of bulk graphite (3.34Å). This can be attributed to the high density of surface groups of CQDs [25]. IR, TGA, and PXRD analyses confirmed that the surfaces of the fabricated CQDs were highly saturated with carboxylic acid functional groups. The morphology and size distribution of CQDs were examined by TEM and DLS-based analyses. TEM images showed that well-organized and spherical shaped CQDs were formed with a size range of 2–5nm (Fig. 1F). The size distribution was confirmed by DLS analysis, showing an average size range of 7.5nm (Fig. S1B). The pH dependent fluorescence behavior of CQDs was also examined (Fig. S1C). A decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed at high and low pH; however, the quenching was more significant at basic rather than acidic pH ranges. Within the pH range of 2–9, more than 80% of the fluorescence intensity remained, in agreement with previously reported results, indicating the applicability of CQDs at a broad pH range [26]. In addition, it was found that the fluorescent intensity of CQDs was not affected by the concentration of salt (Fig. S1D). This indicates that diverse salt concentrations may be employed for the catalytic application of CQDs. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded at regular intervals of time up to 3h in order to examine their time-dependent stability, showing no difference in intensity (Fig. S1E).

The choice of ILs was based on their ability to form micelles in water. The interior cavity of the formed micelles may trap organic reactants to be dissolved, so that the reactions occur in an aqueous colloidal system [27]. In this context, ILs with long chain hydrocarbon moieties were selected. 1-Methyl-3-octyl-1H-imidazol-3-ium (IL-1) and 3-dodecyl-1-methyl-1H-imidazol-3-ium (IL-2) were synthesized using a literature method (Scheme 1 ) [28].

At a certain concentration of CQDs, DLS observations were recorded upon the successive addition of ILs. Typical DLS histograms were observed when the concentration of 195 and 17mM was attained for IL-1 and IL-2, respectively, in accordance with literature values (Fig. S2) [27]. The DLS results showed that the size distribution of the micelles was narrow, within the nanometer range. The average size of the micelles formed by IL-1 and IL-2 was 19 and 24nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of a CQD-CRS hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of CQDs within the micelles resulting in the formation of a CQD-colloidal reaction system (CRS) hybrid system was confirmed by dissolving the CQDs in water and recording the fluorescence emission profiles upon continuous addition of IL-1 and IL-2 (Fig. 2 A and B). When the critical micelle concentration (CMC) (195mM for IL-1 and 17mM for IL-2) was attained, a quenching in the fluorescence intensity was observed. This indicates that once the ILs attain the CMC, the organized micelles are formed. Therefore, the CQDs are entrapped inside the micelle core, finally resulting in the quenching of the fluorescence intensity. AFM images showed the structural changes of micelles upon the addition of CQDs with an increase in size, confirming the interaction of CQDs with micelles and thus, the CQD-CRS hybrid formation (Fig. 2C-F). DLS studies showed that the average size of the micelles formed by IL-1 increased from 19 to 27nm upon the addition of CQDs (Fig. S2A vs 2G). Similarly, micelles formed by IL-2 increased from 26 to 32nm (Fig. S2B vs 2H). These results confirmed the interaction of CQDs with micelles and the formation of the CQD-CRS hybrid.

The effect of the concentration of ILs on the yield of the reaction was evaluated. Experiments were performed using a concentration of ILs above as well as below the CMC, while the concentration of CQDs was maintained constant. The results are summarized in Table 2 . Using concentrations of ILs below the CMC, the yields of the desired product were moderate, i.e., 44 and 47% with IL-1 and IL-2, respectively (entries 1 and 3). With an increase in the concentration of ILs above the CMC, the yields of the product increased to 97 and 98% with IL-1 and IL-2, respectively (entries 2 and 4). These results imply that the micelle formation is a critical step, wherein the reactants are encapsulated. Blank experiments were also performed. The same reaction was carried out in the absence of CQDs, ILs, or both, affording similar results as the reaction performed under CMC, demonstrating that the micelle formation is critical to accelerate the reaction rates (entries 5–10). These results show that both ILs with concentrations above the CMC and the presence of CQDs are essential factors for the catalytic ability of CQD-CRS.

Next, the effect of the temperature on the yield of the product was investigated (Table 3 ). By increasing the reaction temperature from 20 to 40°C, no effect was observed on the yield of the product (entries 1–5). However, as the reaction temperature exceeded 40°C, the yield of the product decreased significantly (entries 6–9). To elucidate the temperature effect on the reaction, DLS analysis of the reaction mixture at different temperatures was performed. The DLS histograms show that at a given concentration of ILs, the micelles remain intact below 40°C. As the temperature rises above 40°C, the micelles are disrupted because of the increased kinetic energy of the molecules.

With the optimized reaction conditions in hand, various 4H-chromene derivatives were synthesized starting from naphthalen-1-ol and naphthalen-2-ol. The results are presented in Tables 4 and 5
. The desired products were precipitated from the corresponding reaction mixtures. Therefore, the simple filtration of the reaction mixture was sufficient to provide the products in a pure state. The yields of the 4H-chromene derivatives were irrespective of the aldehyde and alcohol structures. With substrates having strong electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups, the reaction proceeded smoothly, affording high yields of the products.

For the purpose of characterization, single crystal XRD data for compounds 7 and 8 were collected. The ORTEP diagrams of these compounds are shown in Fig. 3 . The selected bond lengths and bond angles for compounds 7 and 8 are summarized in Tables S2 and S3.

To examine the recyclability of this catalytic system, the filtrate of the reaction mixture was concentrated to the original volume to reach the CMC concentration. A reaction between 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde, 1-naphthol, and malononitrile was carried out using the above solution. The yield of product 1 was found to be 95%. Repeated catalytic experiments were performed by recycling CQD-CRS ten times, as shown in Fig. 4 . It was found that the yields of product 1 were almost the same in all ten runs, showing that the efficacy of the catalytic system was maintained over ten runs.

The EcoScale and E-factor are widely used as green chemistry metrics [29–31]. The EcoScale is a parameter used to evaluate the effectiveness and ecological features of a synthetic process. The E-factor is defined by the mass ratio of waste to product: E-factor=total waste/product [32]. Reactions with a high E-factor have a more negative impact on the environment than reactions with a low E-factor. The greenness of the present method was evaluated by calculating these parameters. This method scored a high EcoScale value of 68.5 with a low E-factor of 0.078, indicating that it is acceptable from a green chemistry point of view (Tables S4 and S5). Moreover, the high yield of products, short reaction time, ease of purification, substrate generality, use of non-toxic solvents, and recyclability of the catalyst system make the present process more desirable than previously reported methods (Table S1).

FT-IR spectrum of 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde in the presence of CQDs showed a longer wavelength shift of the CO vibration (Fig. S3), implying that CQDs activate the CO group of the aldehydes. Upon addition of water until a concentration above the critical micelle concentration range, the ILs organize themselves in the form of micelles. The incorporation of CQDs into the above colloidal system results in the formation of CQD-CRS. Water insoluble reactant molecules diffuse inside the hydrophobic core of CQD-CRS. The entrapped organic molecules are thus efficiently converted into the product owing to the catalytic activity of CQD-CRS upon implementation of a sonothermal source of energy. It is known that CQDs activate aldehydes via hydrogen bonding in nucleophilic addition reactions [33]. In this context, a plausible mechanism is proposed in Scheme 3 . Organic reactants aggregate in the cavity of the micelles due to their hydrophobicity. The CQDs activate the CO group of the aldehyde via hydrogen bonding, accelerating the condensation with malononitrile. The CQDs further activate the resulting condensation product, arylidene malononitrile, which promotes the reaction with naphthol.

We have validated a simple and green solvent based protocol for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives using micelle based nanosynthesizers organized through ionic liquids with the consequent implementation of the catalytic ability of CQDs. The present method has a high EcoScale and low E-factor, demonstrating greenness. The advantages of this method include high yield, reusability of the catalyst, and easy workup. Furthermore, the method employed a simpler sonication based instrumental setup compared to the conventional heating equipment. The green chemistry aspect is also covered by using water as solvent.

For the preparation of CQDs, citric acid (1.0g) was dissolved in distilled water (25mL). The mixture was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 160°C for 6h. Finally, the reactor was allowed to cool to room temperature. The reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min to eliminate the large particles. The transparent blue dispersion obtained was dialyzed against pure water using a dialysis membrane (Da=1000) for one day to remove the unreacted ions and small particles. The optical properties of the resulting CQDs were determined using UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy. The structural properties were characterized by TEM and DLS methods. IR spectroscopy was used to assign the various functional groups associated with CQDs. Powder XRD and TGA analyses were used for examining the surfaces of the fabricated CQDs.

[28] A solution of N-methylimidazole (1.0 equiv) and the respective 1-bromoalkane (1.0 equiv) in acetonitrile was stirred at room temperature. After 8h, the solvent was removed to afford the corresponding IL. The characterization of the ILs was carried out using NMR and mass spectroscopic methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Typical procedure for the synthesis of 4H-chromene derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: A suspension of CQDs (1.0 equiv) in distilled water (100mL) was placed in a beaker. The ILs were added in such a way that the final concentration of ILs remained higher that the CMC to produce CQD-CRS. 1-Naphthol (1.0 equiv), malononitrile (1.0 equiv), and an aldehyde (1.0 equiv) were added to the resulting CQD-CRS. The resulting reaction mixture was sonicated for 15min. The precipitate was filtered and washed three times with water, and finally with a small amount of methanol to provide the pure product.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to IIT Ropar for the fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.mcat.2017.06.032.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
Supporting information for this article is available on the WWW under DOI: (crystallographic data for compounds 7 and 8 (CCDC 1507316 and 1507317) and 1H NMR data for products).
